13: ûḇᵉhaʿălôt hakkōhănîm min haqqōḏeš... wayyāšeb hāʿānān ʾet bêt YHWH — ‘when the priests came out of the holy place... then the house of YHWH was filled with a cloud (ʿānān).’ The trigger for the glory-cloud’s arrival is the Levitical singing of the ḥesed-formula: ‘For he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever (kî lᵉʿôlām ḥasdô).’ The cloud comes in response to worship, not merely to the completion of the building. The ʿānān (cloud) is the standard term for the divine presence-cloud from Exodus 13:21 (wilderness pillar of cloud) through the tabernacle’s filling (Exodus 40:34-35) to this temple-filling. The parallel is deliberate: ‘so that the priests could not stand to minister because of the cloud, for the glory (kāḇôḏ) of YHWH filled the house of God’ (2 Chronicles 5:14) echoes Exodus 40:35: ‘Moses was not able to enter the tent of meeting because the cloud settled on it, and the glory of YHWH filled the tabernacle.’ The Chronicler’s temple-filling deliberately echoes the tabernacle-filling to establish the temple as the tabernacle’s legitimate successor. The MKT renders ʿānān as ‘cloud’ and kāḇôḏ as ‘glory,’ maintaining the standard OT pairing that the NT picks up in the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:5: a bright cloud and a voice) and in Revelation 15:8 (the temple filled with smoke from the divine glory).
14: lōʾ yāḵᵉlû hakkōhănîm laʿămōḏ lᵉšārēt — ‘the priests could not stand to minister.’ The divine glory’s overpowering of the priestly ministry is a recurring OT motif: at Sinai (Exodus 19:21-24: the people could not approach the mountain), at the tabernacle (Exodus 40:35: Moses could not enter), and now at the temple. The inability to stand (laʿămōḏ) contrasts with the Levites’ normal standing-ministry position: 1 Chronicles 23:30 describes the Levites as standing ‘every morning to thank and praise YHWH.’ The glory overcomes the posture of service: normal ministry is impossible in the presence of unmediated divine glory. The NT’s equivalent: at the Transfiguration, the disciples ‘fell on their faces and were terrified’ (Matthew 17:6) — the glory of Christ overwhelms the human capacity to stand. The priestly inability-to-stand is resolved in the NT by the high priest who has entered the Most Holy Place ‘once for all’ (Hebrews 9:12), opening access for those who follow through him.
1: wattišlem kol-hammᵉlāʾkāh ʾăšer-ʿāśāh šᵉlōmōh lᵉḇêt YHWH wayyāḇēʾ šᵉlōmōh ʾet-qŏḏᵉšê dāwîḏ ʾābîw — ‘All the work Solomon did on the house of the LORD was completed. He then brought in what his father David had dedicated.’ wattišlem kol-hammᵉlāʾkāh (all the work was completed/made whole) — the verb šālam (be complete, finished) is the completion-root of šālôm (peace, wholeness): the temple’s completion is described in the language of wholeness and peace. The formula echoes Exodus 39:32 (wattēkel kol-ʿăḇōḏat miškan ʾōhel môʿēḏ, ‘all the work of the tabernacle of the tent of meeting was completed’) and Exodus 40:33 (waykkal Mōšeh ʾet-hammᵉlāʾkāh), establishing the temple’s completion as the fulfilment of the tabernacle-completion pattern. David’s dedicated silver and gold (1 Chronicles 18:7-11; 2 Chronicles 26:26-28) was held in treasury until the permanent building was ready — the transfer marks the new building as the legitimate heir of David’s accumulated sacred resources.
2: ʾāz yaqhēl šᵉlōmōh ʾet-ziqnê yiśrāʾēl wᵉʾet-kol-rāʾšê hammattôt nᵉśîʾê hāʾāḇôt liḇnê yiśrāʾēl el-yᵉrûšālaim — ‘Solomon then summoned to Jerusalem all the elders of Israel and all the tribal heads — the leaders of the Israelite ancestral houses.’ The qahal (assembly) of ‘all Israel’ for the ark-installation ceremony is the covenant community’s supreme gathering. The three-tier leadership structure — elders (zᵉqēnîm), tribal heads (rāʾšê hammattôt), ancestral-house leaders (nᵉśîʾê hāʾāḇôt) — represents the full administrative-genealogical hierarchy of Israel. Gathering all three levels for the ark-installation parallels the Sinai covenant assembly, where elders and leaders came before YHWH (Exodus 24:1,9-11). The Chronicler’s ‘all Israel’ ideal is at its peak in this scene: the twelve-tribe covenant people united before YHWH at his permanent house.
3: wayyiqqāhᵃlû ʾel-hammelek kol-ʾanᵉšê yiśrāʾēl bahāg hûʾ haḥōḏeš haššᵉḇîʿî — ‘All the Israelite men assembled before the king at the feast (ḥag) in the seventh month.’ haḥāg (the festival) in the seventh month is the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot, 15-21 Tishri) — the most joyful and harvest-associated of the three pilgrimage feasts. Its timing for the ark-installation is theologically deliberate: Sukkot commemorates YHWH’s dwelling with Israel in tents in the wilderness, and the ark’s installation in the permanent temple marks the end of the wandering-tent era. The temporary booths of Sukkot celebrate the provisional tabernacle-phase; the permanent temple inaugurated at Sukkot signals the completion of YHWH’s ‘dwelling in tents’ with his people (2 Samuel 7:6) in favour of the permanent house.
4: wayyāḇōʾû kol-ziqnê yiśrāʾēl wayyiśśᵉʾû halᵉwiyyim ʾet-hāʾārôn — ‘When all the elders of Israel had arrived, the Levites took up the ark.’ The Chronicler specifies Levites (halᵉwiyyim) as the ark-bearers, where 1 Kings 8:3 says priests. This is consistent with the Chronicler’s concern that David’s ark-transport protocol (established after Uzzah’s death, 1 Chr 13-15) be followed precisely: in 1 Chronicles 15:2, David declared ‘no one may carry the ark of God except the Levites.’ The Chronicler’s Levite-specification corrects what he may have seen as the Kings account’s imprecision, asserting that the lawful Levitical carriers transported the ark to the temple, not merely priests in general.
5: wayy ᵃʿălû ʾet-hāʾārôn wᵉʾet-ʾōhel môʿēḏ wᵉʾet-kol-kᵉlê haqqōḏeš ʾăšer bāʾōhel — ‘They brought up the ark and the tent of meeting along with all the sacred furnishings inside the tent.’ The deliberate co-transportation of the tabernacle (ʾōhel môʿēḏ) with the ark to the temple signals the transfer of the Mosaic sanctuary’s authority to the Solomonic building. The tent of meeting is not discarded but brought into the temple — its sacred furnishings are integrated into the new structure. The Mosaic era does not simply end; it is absorbed and consummated in the Solomonic temple. The same principle governs the NT’s relationship to the OT: the law is not abolished but fulfilled (Matthew 5:17).
6: wᵉhammelek šᵉlōmōh wᵉkol-ʿăḏat yiśrāʾēl hannᵉʿādîm ʿālāyw lipnê hāʾārôn — ‘King Solomon and the whole congregation of Israel assembled before the ark were sacrificing sheep and cattle so many that they could not be recorded or counted.’ ʾăšer lōʾ-yissāpᵉrû wᵉlōʾ yimmānû (that could not be recorded or counted) — the double negation of counting/recording is an intensified expression of numerical excess. As at David’s ark-procession (1 Chronicles 15:26: sacrifices offered), the occasion calls forth sacrifice beyond enumeration. The uncountable sacrifices match the earlier uncountable bronze (2 Chronicles 4:18) and the silver-like-stones (2 Chronicles 1:15): abundance-beyond-reckoning is the Chronicler’s rhetorical marker for the covenant’s full blessing.
7: wayyāḇîʾû hakkōhᵃnîm ʾet-ʾărôn bᵉrît-YHWH ʾel-mᵉqômô ʾel-dᵉḇîr habbayit ʾel-qōḏeš haqqŏḏāšîm — ‘The priests brought the ark of the LORD’s covenant to its designated place (el-mᵉqômô) — the inner sanctuary of the house, the Most Holy Place.’ ʾel-mᵉqômô (to its place, to its designated position) — the ark has a māqôm (place) prepared for it; this is not ad hoc installation but the fulfillment of a divinely specified spatial arrangement. The entire temple was built to house the ark at this precise location. The dᵉḇîr (inner sanctuary), the qōḏeš haqqŏḏāšîm (Most Holy Place) — the three terms (inner sanctuary + most holy place + beneath the cherubim’ wings, 2 Chronicles 5:8) pile up to maximize the sense of sacred enclosure: the ark is placed in the deepest, holiest, most protected location the covenant people can construct.
8: wayyhyû hakkᵉrûḇîm pōrᵉśîm kᵉnāpayim ʿal-mᵉqôm hāʾārôn waykassû hakkᵉrûḇîm ʿal-hāʾārôn wᵉʿal-baddāyw — ‘The cherubim spread their wings over the ark’s resting place, sheltering the ark and its carrying poles from above.’ pōrᵉśîm kᵉnāpayim ʿal (spreading wings over) — the covering wings represent divine protection. The two large cherubim (2 Chronicles 3:10-13) spread their combined twenty-cubit wingspan over the ark, creating a cherubim-shelter. This mirrors the tabernacle ark’s small gold cherubim (Exodus 25:20: ‘The cherubim shall spread out their wings above, overshadowing the mercy seat with their wings’). The temple-scale cherubim take the tabernacle’s protective-presence motif and scale it to fill the entire room. The carrying poles (baddîm) — kept in the rings so the ark could be transported — are now sheltered under the cherub-wings, signaling the ark’s arrival at its permanent resting place.
9: wayyā'ărîkû habbaddîm wayyērāʾû rāʾšê habbaddîm min-hāʾārôn ʿal-pᵉnê haddᵉḇîr wᵉlōʾ yērāʾû haḥûṣāh — ‘The poles were long enough that their ends could be seen from the holy place just outside the inner sanctuary, but not from outside.’ The poles’ visibility from the Holy Place is a detail confirming both the poles’ length and the ark’s positioning relative to the curtain. The poles were never to be removed (Exodus 25:15: ‘the poles shall remain in the rings of the ark; they shall not be taken out’). Their visibility through the curtain — without being visible to lay Israelites outside — marks the boundary of priestly access: the priests who entered the Holy Place could see the poles, but the laity could not. The poles are thus a sign to the priesthood alone of the ark’s presence and continued portability-in-principle even in its permanent location.
10: ʾên bāʾārôn raq šnê hallûḥôt ʾăšer nātan Mōšeh baḥōrēḇ — ‘Nothing was in the ark except the two stone tablets Moses had placed there at Horeb, where the LORD made a covenant with the Israelites after they came out of Egypt.’ The negative inventory — only the tablets, nothing else — addresses the reader’s potential question about the jar of manna and Aaron’s staff (Exodus 16:33-34; Numbers 17:10; Hebrews 9:4). The Chronicler’s ‘only the tablets’ reflects the actual first-century temple understanding that the ark contained only the Decalogue tablets. The two tablets are the covenant document itself — lûḥôt hāʾeben (stone tablets), the written record of the Sinai covenant. The ark is literally the covenant-container: the throne of YHWH’s presence is built on and around the covenant document, as the NT’s equivalent is built on and around the word of God (Hebrews 8:6; 2 Corinthians 3:3).
11: wayᵉhî bᵉṣēʾt hakkōhᵃnîm min-haqqōḏeš kî kol-hakkōhᵃnîm hammᵉṣūʾîm hitqadddāšû — ‘When the priests came out of the Holy Place — all the priests present had consecrated themselves regardless of their division.’ hitqaddᵃšû (they had consecrated themselves) — the Hithpael reflexive of qāḏaš (to be holy): the priests performed their own self-consecration rituals, actively making themselves holy for this unique occasion. lōʾ lᵉmaḥlᵉqôtêhem (not according to their divisions) — the normal rota of priestly divisions (established by David in 1 Chr 24) is suspended for the ark-installation: all priests participate simultaneously, regardless of whose course it is. The full priestly cohort functions as one body for this unrepeatable event.
12: wᵉhallᵉwiyyim hammᵉšōrᵃrîm lᵉkullām lᵉʾāsāp lᵉhêmān lîḏûtûn — ‘and all the Levitical musicians — Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun, with their sons and relatives, dressed in fine linen, standing east of the altar with cymbals, lyres, and harps.’ The three guilds of Levitical musicians — Asaph, Heman, Jeduthun — were established by David (1 Chronicles 25:1-31) for the permanent temple music program. Their positioning east of the altar (the approach-direction to the temple) places them at the threshold between the congregation and the sanctuary. bûṣ (fine linen) is the priestly textile: by wearing linen, the Levitical musicians adopt the priestly garment for this occasion, blurring the Levite-priest distinction at the moment of maximum sacred convergence. With them were 120 priests playing trumpets (meṣôltayim, cymbals; nᵉḇālîm, harps; kinnōrôt, lyres) — the full Davidic orchestral apparatus deployed for the most significant covenant-worship event since Sinai.
